posts.name: Arv R@()
posts.cooked:
Book Review:
Burgundy: a Comprehensive Guide to the Producers, Appellations, and Wines
Written by Robert M Parker, Jr.
Maps by Jeanyee Wong
Drawings by Christopher Wormell
1990 publication, 1st Edition, 1051 pgs

Robert Parker was an important wine critic of another era, who was generally ignored when it came to the wines of Burgundy. This book helps show why that happened, even if that was not its intent. I should mention: I am not a regular pinot or Burgundy consumer, maybe enjoying those once per month or so, so consider that aspect when looking at this post. This book was read more for background knowledge rather than it affecting my cellar stocking. NB this book is very old; the 1990 publication date means that it was really written during 1988 or so, as the 1989 vintage is just being discussed in terms of weather. And the work feels dated, since the author’s rules of thumbs and impressions were built upon their experiences of the late 60’s into the early 80’s.

The book is laid out differently from typical wine books. It leads off with a conventional history of the region and then segues into Parker’s views on the region/wines for 25 pages, at least – maybe more if one considers how those same ideas are also infused into other aspects. His views are strident, and generally inflected by a gestalt that ‘Bordeaux is better’. It comes across in many ways, and seems strange for a book about a different region/varietals. Some opinions include suggestions like: don’t decant, don’t cellar most producers, vineyards aren’t important, and so on. Just to note: I don’t have informed opinions on those topics when it comes to Burgundy but I suspect those would be grating to stakeholders in the Burgundy ecosystem, along with collectors. And the overall tone is jarring too. I’ve read all the other Parker books over the decades, multiple editions too, and this one really sticks out this way. Maybe his editor gave him too much leeway here.

The next bulk of the book covers producers, which comes before the AOC. In most wine related books I can recall, regions/villages tend to be discussed, with a focus on notable producers within the area at the end of the section. Perhaps the overall Burgundian negociant model makes that layout too hard to do here, but if one is not knowledgeable already about the wines/nomenclature, I think this makes it even more confusing. After the villages description, there are vintage descriptions (highly generalized for the whites) and recycled WA reviews for the recent (1980-1989) reds. For the latter parts, the scores are quite conservative, but in my miniscule sampling/memory of what was rated, I liked the scant few wines I had cross tasted more than he did.

PXL_20221014_220956233~21920×2504 486 KB

The maps and drawings are excellent, and are the main reason to keep a copy on the shelf, as a reference guide. They seem to be more detailed than what is in recent Hugh Johnson’s Wine Atlas, although they may not be as current. So it’s certainly possible that some spots may have been promoted to 1er cru status, over the years. But I suspect in a fully planted region like Burgundy, these maps are still valid.

I should mention that many wine reviews and scores are harsh. Plenty of wines get scores in the 50’s and 60’s and are deemed undrinkable; certain producers and vintages are basically assessed as ‘do not drink nor purchase’. In RMP’s coverage of other regions over the years, both in the newsletters and books, I don’t recall this negativity. Now its certainly possible that no other region has this kind of variance between great and mediocre vintners, but that seems like a stretch to me. After reading this, I can see why his professional opportunities to taste with makers in Burgundy cellars began to be curtailed. Although I can intellectually understand the value of feedback, if one’s life’s work is getting criticized – especially by one who may not understand it – it becomes easier to decline an appointment.

The book is long, and repetitive in many instances, and it took more than a month to read since it felt like a chore. Getting motivated to pen a tepid review took just as long afterwards, since I didn’t feel like mentally revisiting this. Overall, about the only value I can see a current owner getting from this is enjoying the nice maps. Maybe that merits a C+ overall. Too much of the rest is likely to be out of date now, or worse, very idiosyncratic views that may not align with prevailing ideas. What is interesting, from close reading of many sections, it is clear how much the author knows about the vineyards, producers, and various issues/nuances/politics of the area - but it feels like conclusions are always forced back to a certain doctrine (no filtering, yields should be lower, etc.)

Used copies change hands for $5-$8 on eBay, Amazon etc. which is what I think its worth. I still have Clive Coate’s (1995 edition) as well as Remington’s Norman’s books to read on Burgundy, so it will be interesting to triangulate with other writers. Thanks for making it to the end!

posts.name: maureen nelson
posts.cooked:
As I recall, he “saved” his reviews of the 1988 vintage reds for the book and at the time they were rather favorable. They were reviews from barrel. Then a couple years went by, the bottled wines arrived in the states but lo and behold the fruit had gone to sleep, leaving the acids and tannins too apparent. What! Tannin in pinot??! Acid in red wine??!

As i recall, he published an “apology” in the newsletter and greatly downgraded his overall assessment of 1988 Burgundy reds. Addy Bassin had bought a ton of 88s and next thing you know, the store couldn’t sell them. That meant for some really great dump bin buying for some of us who loved ‘88 reds. Patience was rewarded as the wines turned out beautifully.

John Gilman and I bonded 25 years ago over our shared love of ‘88 red burgundies.

posts.name: Gaudissabois Johan
posts.cooked:
Friends,

From the start PARKER was not enthousiastic about the 88 vintage and even preferred 87. So what I read here that he changed opinion from being positive towards rather negative IS NOT TRUE. Of course Coates and Meadows are far greater experts on Burgundy but to me the tome written by parker was the first comprehensive study on the region. I liked it. Positive was also that he rated the winemakers , something which most others do not dare (except Coates of course). Even if he, to my total disbelief, awarded 5 stars to Philip Leclerc. Still :I learned a lot. Thank you Robert even if your study is now hopelessly outdated. But then so is MATT KRAMER’s book on Burgundy ( in which he rightly attacked the Guy ACCAD-approach…)…and so many others.
SINCERELY JOHAN

posts.name: Karl K@zaks
posts.cooked:
I bought 88 Rousseau CSJ from Bassins off the (back) shelf in 1999.

Oh was it good!

posts.name: alan weinberg
posts.cooked: in Coates’ Vine he declared 88 Rousseau Chambertin the greatest Chambertin he had ever tasted, about 1990 or 1991, on release.

posts.name: maureen nelson
posts.cooked:
While it is entirely possible what I wrote above is incorrect, it is consistent with my recollection. Unfortunately I no longer possess a copy of that book, nor any old issues. Perhaps someone who does could look.

Mind you, this was before grade inflation so don’t expect 93+ scores.

posts.name: Mikko Tuomi
posts.cooked:
Arv_R:

His views are strident, and generally inflected by a gestalt that ‘Bordeaux is better’.

Sounds like he got one thing right then…

posts.name: Greg Pierce
posts.cooked: I recently bought a used copy of this book as well. Plenty of unintended comedy in it. Reading it confirmed the conclusion of many that Parker didn’t understand Burgundy and should be ignored with respect to it. I mean, he claims that top Côte d’Or reds and whites should be consumed before 10 years after the vintage. He comments that burgundies often hit their peak of maturity in ~5 years and then “unceremoniously begin to fade after another 6 or 7 months.”

posts.name: alan weinberg
posts.cooked:
Greg_Pierce:

I recently bought a used copy of this book as well. Plenty of unintended comedy in it. Reading it confirmed the conclusion of many that Parker didn’t understand Burgundy and should be ignored with respect to it. I mean, he claims that top Côte d’Or reds and whites should be consumed before 10 years after the vintage. He comments that burgundies often hit their peak of maturity in ~5 years and then “unceremoniously begin to fade after another 6 or 7 months. ”

yes, but as I have often opined, this helped many of us. There was no Burghound, Kelley, or Gilman, just small audiences for Kolm, Tanzer, and Coates. So Parker and later Rovani kept Burg prices down for the rest of us. Who would want those weedy acidic wines that fade in months?

posts.name: Tom Blach
posts.cooked:
Gaudissabois_Johan:

he rightly attacked the Guy ACCAD-approach

Accad was unquestionably one of the fathers of modern burgundy, in the vineyard more than in the cellar. Not all the wines the making of which he was involved with were succesful though it seems many have stood the test of time far better than expected. Does anybody know what happened to him?

posts.name: Gaudissabois Johan
posts.cooked:
Tom,

Don’t know what happened to him but i do know that GRIVOT’s wines are far better now than they were in 88 and the couple of years after that where he folllowed ACCAD’s advice to the hilt…

Sincerely JOHAN

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
It’s definitely interesting to reread this book, along with others from the era, thirty-plus years later. Anthony Hanson’s declaration that “great Burgundy smells of sh*t”, removed from later editions of his 1982 book, probably captures better than anything how much times have changed in the interim.

In one of his last interviews, as relayed by Andrew Jefford in Decanter IIRC, RP comments that in retrospect he feels he was too bellicose in Burgundy, and that he underestimated the capacity to age and improve of lighter, more elegantly-styled vintages of red Burgundy. I think we can all agree with that.

But while it’s easy to focus on what authors don’t say or get wrong, several of the key take-home messages of the book do seem to me, in the end, to have stood the test of time, in particular: that the problem of overcropping, enabled by intensive agrochemical usage, had diminished the quality and concentration of the grapes in Burgundy and their capacity to ripen; that over-chaptalization was a poor substitute for moderate yields and better maturity; and that aggressive filtration (filtration technology being far from what it is today) was eviscerating many wines. I think we could all agree that the red Burgundies of the 1980s are hardly wines to buy blindly regardless of vintage or producer.

He also correctly identified more than a few producers who were far from the household names they are today. Jasper Morris’ 1988 book on White Burgundy, for example, makes no mention of Coche-Dury (unless I missed it - I haven’t looked at it in a decade), whereas RP singles him out as one of region’s best. Similarly, Lalou Bize-Leroy has often mentioned to me that she credits RP’s early praise of her wines for the fact that her new domaine was taken seriously. A lot of things like this look self-evident in 2022, but where perhaps not quite so obvious back in the day, even if serious Burgundy lovers were often in many cases already tuned in, of course.

posts.name: Marcu$ Stanley
posts.cooked:
That’s hilarious – he was clearly mistaking the shut down period of the wine for the end of its drinkability!

It’s kind of common I think for people who come in liking more obvious wines to have a hard time understanding Burgundy at first. I had the same issue early on. I guess his shoot from the hip/I like it or I don’t style, which served him so well in calling out Bordeaux producers who weren’t living up to their name, really betrayed him here.

posts.name: Tom Blach
posts.cooked: True, but they are much better now than in his post-Accad period also. On the other hand it has surprising how well the Grivot Accad wines have aged, my few recent experiences have been really enjoyable.
